expanded_description: Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon double bond ($\text{C}=\text{C}$). This section explores their unique structure, $sp^2$ hybridization, trigonal planar geometry, and systematic IUPAC nomenclature rules that distinguish them from saturated alkanes.

core_concept.principle: The Carbon-Carbon Double Bond Defines Alkene Structure and Reactivity
core_concept.explanation: Alkenes contain one or more $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bonds, consisting of one strong $\sigma$ (sigma) bond and one weaker $\pi$ (pi) bond. The $\pi$-bond forms from sideways overlap of unhybridized $p$ orbitals, creating electron density above and below the bond axis. This structure makes alkenes more reactive than alkanes and restricts rotation around the double bond, enabling geometric isomerism.

image.alt: Diagram showing alkene double bond with sigma and pi components
image.caption: The $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond: one $\sigma$-bond (head-on overlap) + one $\pi$-bond (sideways $p$-orbital overlap).

sections.introduction: Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons characterized by the presence of at least one carbon-carbon double bond ($\text{C}=\text{C}$). The term 'unsaturated' indicates that these molecules contain fewer hydrogen atoms than the maximum possible for their carbon count, due to the presence of $\pi$-bonds. Alkenes are also known as 'olefins' (from 'olefiant gas', meaning oil-forming gas) because many form oily products when reacted with halogens. They serve as crucial feedstocks for polymer production and organic synthesis.

key_concept.definition: An alkene is an unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one carbon-carbon double bond, with general molecular formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}$ for acyclic alkenes with one double bond. Each double-bonded carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized with trigonal planar geometry.
key_concept.context: The double bond consists of one $\sigma$-bond and one $\pi$-bond. The $\pi$-bond is weaker and more reactive than the $\sigma$-bond, making alkenes undergo addition reactions rather than substitution reactions typical of alkanes.

image.alt: General structure of alkenes showing double bond
image.caption: Alkene general structure: $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}$ with at least one $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond.

principles.statement: For acyclic alkenes with exactly one double bond, the general molecular formula is $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}$, containing two fewer hydrogens than the corresponding alkane ($\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$).
principles.application: This formula allows quick identification of alkene structures from molecular formulas. Each double bond or ring reduces hydrogen count by 2 relative to the saturated alkane. Multiple double bonds further reduce hydrogen count: $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2}$ for two double bonds (dienes).
principles.analogy: Think of saturation like a sponge fully soaked with water (alkanes). Removing two hydrogens to create a double bond is like squeezing out some water—the molecule is now 'unsaturated' and can absorb more atoms through addition reactions.
principles.example: Ethene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$): $n=2$, so $\text{H} = 2n = 4$. Compare to ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$): the double bond removes 2 hydrogens.

principles.statement: The $\pi$-bond component of the $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond prevents free rotation, locking substituents into fixed spatial arrangements.
principles.application: This restriction enables geometric (cis-trans) isomerism, where different spatial arrangements of substituents create distinct compounds with different physical and chemical properties. Rotation would require breaking the $\pi$-bond ($\sim 264 \text{ kJ/mol}$ energy barrier).
principles.analogy: Imagine two people shaking hands ($\sigma$-bond) while also linking arms ($\pi$-bond). They can't rotate relative to each other without breaking the arm link. This locked position creates distinct 'same side' vs 'opposite side' arrangements.
principles.example: In 2-butene, methyl groups can be on the same side (cis-2-butene) or opposite sides (trans-2-butene) of the double bond—these are different compounds with different boiling points.

components.definition: The alkene functional group consists of two $sp^2$ hybridized carbon atoms connected by one $\sigma$-bond and one $\pi$-bond, with bond angles of approximately $120^\circ$ in trigonal planar geometry.
components.interpretation: The $\sigma$-bond ($\sim 350 \text{ kJ/mol}$) is stronger than the $\pi$-bond ($\sim 264 \text{ kJ/mol}$). The weaker $\pi$-bond is more easily broken, making it the reactive site for addition reactions. Total double bond energy is less than twice a single bond energy.

image.alt: Diagram showing sigma and pi bond components in alkene
image.caption: Alkene double bond: $\sigma$-bond (blue, head-on overlap) + $\pi$-bond (red, sideways $p$-orbital overlap).

sections.introduction: Understanding alkene structure requires detailed knowledge of $sp^2$ hybridization—the orbital mixing that creates the trigonal planar geometry around double-bonded carbons. This hybridization pattern determines bond angles, molecular shape, and the formation of the $\pi$-bond that defines alkene reactivity.

classifications.value: Found in all carbon atoms participating in $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bonds
characteristics: Three $sp^2$ hybrid orbitals + one unhybridized $p$ orbital
Forms one $\pi$-bond + two $\sigma$-bonds per double-bonded carbon
classifications.behavior: The three $sp^2$ orbitals lie in one plane (trigonal planar), forming $\sigma$-bonds with bond angles of $120^\circ$. The unhybridized $p$ orbital is perpendicular to this plane and overlaps sideways with a neighboring $p$ orbital to create the $\pi$-bond.

examples.explanation: Each carbon uses its $2s$ orbital and two $2p$ orbitals to form three $sp^2$ hybrids. These form $\sigma$-bonds: two $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds and one $\text{C}-\text{C}$ $\sigma$-bond. The remaining unhybridized $2p$ orbital on each carbon overlaps sideways to form the $\pi$-bond.

image.alt: Ethene showing sp2 hybridization and orbital overlap
image.caption: Ethene: each carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized with trigonal planar geometry ($120^\circ$ bond angles).

examples.explanation: The two double-bonded carbons are $sp^2$ hybridized; the methyl carbon ($\text{CH}_3$) is $sp^3$ hybridized. This creates a molecule with mixed hybridization states.

classifications.value: Head-on orbital overlap creating strong, localized bonds
characteristics: Electron density concentrated along bond axis
Allows free rotation in single bonds but not in double bonds
classifications.behavior: In alkenes, $\sigma$-bonds form the molecular skeleton: $\text{C}-\text{C}$ $\sigma$-bond between double-bonded carbons, and $\text{C}-\text{H}$ $\sigma$-bonds to attached hydrogens or substituents.

examples.explanation: Five $\sigma$-bonds total: one $\text{C}-\text{C}$ ($sp^2$-$sp^2$) and four $\text{C}-\text{H}$ ($sp^2$-$s$). These create the rigid planar structure.

image.caption: Ethene $\sigma$-framework: all atoms lie in one plane with $120^\circ$ bond angles.

classifications.value: Sideways $p$-orbital overlap creating electron density above and below bond axis
characteristics: Electron density in two lobes (above and below plane)
classifications.behavior: The $\pi$-bond forms after the $\sigma$-bond framework is established. The two $p$ orbitals must be parallel for effective overlap, which locks the molecular geometry and creates the planar structure around the double bond.

examples.explanation: Two parallel $2p$ orbitals (one on each carbon) overlap sideways, creating electron density in two lobes: one above and one below the molecular plane. This $\pi$-electron cloud is exposed and reactive.

image.caption: $\pi$-bond: sideways $p$-orbital overlap creates electron density above and below the $\sigma$-bond axis.

sections.key_concept: Alkene carbons are $sp^2$ hybridized: three $sp^2$ orbitals form $\sigma$-bonds in a plane ($120^\circ$ angles); one unhybridized $p$ orbital forms the $\pi$-bond perpendicular to this plane.

sections.introduction: IUPAC nomenclature for alkenes follows similar principles to alkane naming but with critical modifications to indicate the presence and position of the double bond. The suffix changes from '-ane' to '-ene', and the chain must be numbered to give the double bond the lowest possible position number.

key_concept.definition: Alkene IUPAC names follow the format: [Substituents]-[Double Bond Position]-[Parent Chain]-ene. The parent chain must contain the double bond, and numbering prioritizes the double bond position over substituent positions.
key_concept.context: For alkenes with 2 or 3 carbons, position numbers are unnecessary (ethene, propene) because the double bond can only be in one position. For 4+ carbons, the double bond position must be specified.

image.caption: Alkene naming: substituent positions + double bond position + parent name with '-ene' suffix.

principles.statement: The parent chain must be the longest continuous carbon chain that contains the double bond, even if a longer chain without the double bond exists.
principles.application: This rule ensures the functional group (double bond) is always part of the parent name. A 6-carbon chain with the double bond takes priority over a 7-carbon chain without it.
principles.analogy: Think of the double bond as the 'VIP' of the molecule—it must be included in the main party (parent chain) even if there's a bigger room (longer chain) available elsewhere.
principles.example: In a molecule with a 7-carbon chain (no double bond) and a 6-carbon chain (with double bond), the 6-carbon chain is the parent: hexene, not heptane.

principles.principle: Numbering Priority: Double Bond Over Substituents
principles.statement: The parent chain is numbered from the end that gives the double bond the lowest possible position number, even if this results in higher substituent numbers.
principles.application: Double bond position takes priority over substituent positions. Only when double bond positions are equal from both ends do substituent positions determine numbering direction.
principles.analogy: Like addressing a letter: the street name (double bond) is more important than the house number (substituent). Get the street right first, then optimize the house number.
principles.example: 4-methylpent-1-ene (double bond at 1) is correct, NOT 2-methylpent-4-ene (double bond at 4), even though 2 < 4 for the methyl group.

principles.statement: The suffix '-ene' replaces '-ane', and the double bond position is indicated by a number before the suffix (e.g., pent-2-ene) or before the parent name (e.g., 2-pentene).
principles.application: Current IUPAC recommendations prefer pent-2-ene format (position before suffix), but 2-pentene is still widely accepted. For multiple double bonds, use '-diene', '-triene', etc., with all positions specified.
principles.analogy: The '-ene' suffix is like a flag marking 'double bond here!' The position number tells you exactly which carbon pair hosts this flag.
principles.example: But-1-ene ($\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$) vs but-2-ene ($\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$)—different compounds with different properties.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Simple Alkene

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the longest chain containing the double bond.
Number from the end giving the double bond the lowest position.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: double bond at 3. Right-to-left: double bond at 2. Choose 2.}$

step_by_step.explanation: Check for substituents: none present in this molecule.
Assemble the name with double bond position.

worked_examples.final_answer: pent-2-ene. The double bond is at position 2, giving the lowest possible number.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Double bond position takes priority in numbering; suffix '-ene' indicates alkene family.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Branched Alkene

step_by_step.explanation: Find longest chain containing the double bond.
Number for lowest double bond position.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: double bond at 3. Right-to-left: double bond at 2. Choose 2.}$

step_by_step.explanation: Identify substituent position with chosen numbering.
Assemble name: substituent + double bond position + parent.

worked_examples.final_answer: 4-methylpent-2-ene. Double bond position (2) takes priority over substituent position (4).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Double bond numbering priority over substituents; alphabetical ordering for multiple substituents.

image.caption: 4-Methylpent-2-ene: 5-carbon parent with double bond at C2 and methyl at C4.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Diene (Two Double Bonds)

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain containing both double bonds.
Number from end giving lowest set of double bond positions.
Use '-diene' suffix for two double bonds; specify both positions.
Assemble complete name.

worked_examples.final_answer: penta-1,3-diene. Both double bond positions must be specified; '-diene' indicates two double bonds.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple double bonds use '-diene', '-triene' suffixes; all positions must be numbered.

sections.introduction: Alkenes can be represented using various structural formats, each serving different purposes. Understanding these representations is essential for visualizing the planar geometry around double bonds and communicating alkene structures accurately.

categories.description: Shows only atom counts (e.g., $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8$ for butene).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Molecular formulas indicate unsaturation but cannot distinguish between structural isomers. $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8$ could represent but-1-ene, but-2-ene, 2-methylpropene, or cyclobutane.

examples.explanation: $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8$ represents multiple structures: but-1-ene, cis-but-2-ene, trans-but-2-ene, 2-methylpropene, cyclobutane. Additional structural information is needed to distinguish them.

image.caption: $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8$ has multiple isomers: structural and geometric variants.

categories.description: Shows atom connectivity with double bond indicated by '=' (e.g., $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}=\text{CHCH}_3$).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Condensed formulas show the sequence of atoms and double bond position but don't clearly show geometry. The '=' symbol indicates the $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond location.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}=\text{CHCH}_3$ shows 4-carbon chain with double bond between carbons 2 and 3. Does not distinguish cis vs trans.

image.caption: Condensed formula shows connectivity but not geometric isomerism.

categories.description: Shows all atoms and bonds explicitly, including the double bond as two lines.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lewis structures display every bond and atom, clearly showing the double bond as two parallel lines. Useful for understanding bonding but becomes complex for larger molecules.

examples.explanation: All 6 atoms shown with bonds: $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond (two lines) and four $\text{C}-\text{H}$ single bonds. All atoms lie in one plane.

image.caption: Ethene Lewis structure showing all bonds and planar geometry.

categories.description: Simplified representation where the double bond is shown as two parallel lines.
categories.detailed_mechanism: In skeletal structures: (1) vertices and endpoints = carbon atoms, (2) hydrogens on carbons are implied, (3) double bonds shown as '=' or two parallel lines, (4) geometry around double bond should be drawn with $120^\circ$ angles to reflect trigonal planar structure.

examples.explanation: Drawn as zigzag line with double line between carbons 2 and 3. Bond angles around double bond drawn as $120^\circ$ to show planar geometry.

image.caption: Hex-2-ene skeletal: double bond shown as parallel lines with $120^\circ$ angles.

sections.introduction: Alkene physical properties are similar to alkanes of comparable molecular weight but show some distinctive trends due to the presence of the $\pi$-bond and planar geometry. Understanding these properties helps predict behavior in separation and purification processes.

categories.description: Alkene boiling points increase with molecular weight, similar to alkanes.
categories.detailed_mechanism: London dispersion forces dominate intermolecular attractions. Each $\text{CH}_2$ addition raises boiling point by $\approx 20-30^\circ\text{C}$. Alkenes have slightly higher boiling points than corresponding alkanes due to increased polarizability from $\pi$-electrons.

examples.explanation: Ethene ($-103.7^\circ\text{C}$) < Propene ($-47.6^\circ\text{C}$) < But-1-ene ($-6.3^\circ\text{C}$) < Pent-1-ene ($30^\circ\text{C}$) < Hex-1-ene ($63^\circ\text{C}$). C2-C4 are gases; C5+ are liquids at room temperature.

image.alt: Graph of alkene boiling points vs carbon number
image.caption: Alkene boiling points increase with chain length, similar to alkanes.

categories.description: Cis and trans isomers have different physical properties due to molecular shape.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Trans isomers are more symmetrical and pack more efficiently in solid state (higher melting points). Cis isomers have net dipole moments from substituent arrangement (higher boiling points due to dipole-dipole interactions).

examples.explanation: cis-But-2-ene: $\text{BP} = 3.7^\circ\text{C}$, $\text{MP} = -138.9^\circ\text{C}$. trans-But-2-ene: $\text{BP} = 0.9^\circ\text{C}$, $\text{MP} = -105.5^\circ\text{C}$. Cis has higher BP (dipole); trans has higher MP (packing).

image.alt: Comparison of cis and trans isomer properties
image.caption: Cis vs trans isomers: different physical properties from different molecular shapes.

categories.description: Alkenes are nonpolar and immiscible with water, similar to alkanes.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Alkenes cannot form hydrogen bonds with water. Densities range from $0.6-0.8 \text{ g/mL}$, less than water ($1.0 \text{ g/mL}$), so alkenes float on water. Soluble in nonpolar organic solvents (hexane, benzene, ether).

examples.explanation: Hex-1-ene ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}$) forms separate layer on water. Density = $0.67 \text{ g/mL}$. Used in organic extractions similar to alkanes.

sections.introduction: Several simple alkenes have major industrial importance as feedstocks for polymer production, chemical synthesis, and fuel additives. Understanding these common alkenes provides context for why alkene chemistry is practically significant.

components.function: Most important organic feedstock; precursor to polyethylene, ethanol, ethylene oxide
components.key_feature: Simplest alkene; plant hormone (fruit ripening); global production > 150 million tons/year

components.function: Polypropylene production; acrylonitrile for synthetic fibers; propylene oxide
components.key_feature: Second most produced alkene; $\text{BP} = -47.6^\circ\text{C}$; used in automotive plastics

components.function: Co-monomer for linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE); butadiene production
Butyl rubber production; methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE) fuel additive
components.key_feature: Branched alkene; more reactive than linear isomers due to substituted double bond; $\text{BP} = -6.9^\circ\text{C}$
Biological significance: building block of natural rubber, steroids, vitamins; emitted by plants

sections.introduction: Advanced alkene nomenclature involves handling multiple double bonds, substituents, and potential ambiguities. This section provides systematic practice with increasingly complex structures to build naming proficiency.

worked_examples.title: Naming with Multiple Substituents

step_by_step.explanation: Find longest chain containing the double bond.
Number for lowest double bond position.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: double bond at 3. Right-to-left: double bond at 3. Equal!}$

step_by_step.explanation: Tiebreaker: number for lowest substituent positions.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: methyl at 3. Right-to-left: methyl at 4. Choose 3.}$

step_by_step.explanation: Assemble name with substituent and double bond position.

worked_examples.final_answer: 3-methylhex-3-ene. Double bond at position 3 from both ends; substituent position (3 vs 4) breaks tie.
worked_examples.concept_applied: When double bond positions are equal, use substituent positions as tiebreaker for numbering direction.

image.caption: 3-Methylhex-3-ene: 6-carbon parent with double bond and methyl both at C3.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Triene (Three Double Bonds)

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain containing all double bonds.
Number from end giving lowest set of double bond positions.
Use '-triene' suffix for three double bonds; specify all positions.
Assemble complete name.

worked_examples.final_answer: hexa-1,3,5-triene. Conjugated triene with alternating double-single bonds; symmetric structure.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple double bonds: '-triene' suffix; all positions specified; conjugated systems have special stability.

worked_examples.title: From Name to Structure: 4-Ethyl-2-Methylhept-2-ene

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain from base name: 'hept-2-ene' = 7 carbons with double bond at position 2.
Number the chain from left to right for drawing.
Add substituents at specified positions: methyl at C2, ethyl at C4.
Add hydrogens to satisfy tetravalency (each carbon needs 4 bonds).
Verify: count total carbons = 7 (parent) + 1 (methyl) + 2 (ethyl) = 10 carbons.

worked_examples.final_answer: Condensed structure: $\text{CH}_3\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)=\text{CHCH}(\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3)\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$. Molecular formula: $\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{20}$ (follows $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}$ for one double bond).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Name-to-structure conversion: parent chain with double bond first, then add substituents at specified positions, then saturate with hydrogens.

image.caption: 4-Ethyl-2-methylhept-2-ene: heptene parent with methyl at C2 and ethyl at C4.

sections.section_title: Alkene Nomenclature Summary and Common Mistakes
sections.introduction: A consolidated summary of alkene naming rules helps prevent common errors. This section highlights critical rules, frequent mistakes, and provides a quick-reference checklist for naming any alkene structure.

principles.statement: Alkene nomenclature differs from alkane nomenclature in three key ways: suffix (-ene vs -ane), parent chain selection (must contain double bond), and numbering priority (double bond over substituents).
principles.application: When naming any hydrocarbon, first identify the functional group. If a double bond exists, apply alkene rules even if a longer alkane chain is present.
principles.analogy: Alkane naming is like naming a street by its length. Alkene naming is like naming a street by its landmark (the double bond)—the landmark takes priority over length.
principles.example: Alkane: 3-methylhexane (longest chain = 6). Alkene: 4-methylhex-2-ene (chain must contain double bond, numbered for double bond position).

principles.statement: Current IUPAC recommendations place the double bond position immediately before the '-ene' suffix (e.g., pent-2-ene), though the older format (2-pentene) remains acceptable.
principles.application: Use pent-2-ene format for consistency with modern IUPAC standards. For multiple double bonds: penta-1,3-diene (all positions before suffix).
principles.analogy: Think of the position number as a label attached directly to the '-ene' flag: 'pent-2-ene' means 'pentane with -ene flag at position 2'.

categories.description: Choosing the longest chain instead of the chain containing the double bond.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students often default to alkane rules and select the absolute longest carbon chain. For alkenes, the chain MUST contain the double bond even if shorter.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 2-propylpent-1-ene (7-carbon chain selected). CORRECT: 3-methylenehexane or proper naming with 6-carbon chain containing double bond. The double bond must be in the parent.

categories.description: Numbering for lowest substituent position instead of lowest double bond position.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Alkane habit of minimizing substituent numbers conflicts with alkene rule of minimizing double bond numbers. Double bond position always takes priority.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 4-methylpent-3-ene (numbered for methyl at 2). CORRECT: 2-methylpent-2-ene (numbered for double bond at 2). Double bond position 2 < 3, so this wins.

categories.type: Common Mistake: Missing Double Bond Position
categories.description: Omitting the double bond position number for alkenes with 4+ carbons.
categories.detailed_mechanism: For ethene and propene, position numbers are unnecessary (only one possible position). For butene and larger, the position MUST be specified to distinguish isomers.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: pentene (ambiguous—which position?). CORRECT: pent-1-ene or pent-2-ene (specific position required). Butene has two isomers; pentene has multiple isomers.

categories.type: Common Mistake: Incorrect Suffix for Multiple Double Bonds
categories.description: Using '-ene' instead of '-diene' or '-triene' for multiple double bonds.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Each double bond must be accounted for in the suffix. Two double bonds = '-diene'; three = '-triene'. All positions must be specified.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: pent-1,3-ene (wrong suffix). CORRECT: penta-1,3-diene (correct suffix for two double bonds). The 'di-' indicates two double bonds.

components.composition: Longest continuous chain containing the double bond
components.key_feature: Must contain double bond even if shorter chain exists without it

components.composition: Assign 1, 2, 3... from end nearest double bond
components.function: Gives double bond the lowest possible position number
components.key_feature: Double bond position priority over substituent positions

components.composition: Alkyl groups: methyl, ethyl, propyl, etc.
components.function: Identifies all branches off parent chain
components.key_feature: Use di-, tri-, tetra- for multiples; alphabetize substituents
Commas between numbers; hyphens between numbers and letters; no spaces

summary: Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond, with general formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}$ for acyclic mono-alkenes. Each double-bonded carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized with trigonal planar geometry ($120^\circ$ bond angles). The double bond consists of one $\sigma$-bond (strong, head-on overlap) and one $\pi$-bond (weaker, sideways $p$-orbital overlap). The $\pi$-bond restricts rotation, enabling geometric (cis-trans) isomerism, and makes alkenes more reactive than alkanes. IUPAC nomenclature requires: (1) parent chain must contain the double bond, (2) numbering prioritizes lowest double bond position over substituent positions, (3) suffix '-ene' replaces '-ane', (4) double bond position specified for $\text{C}_4+$ alkenes (e.g., pent-2-ene). Multiple double bonds use '-diene', '-triene' suffixes with all positions numbered. Common alkenes include ethene (polyethylene feedstock), propene (polypropylene), and butadiene (synthetic rubber). Physical properties resemble alkanes but geometric isomers show different boiling/melting points. Critical naming mistakes to avoid: selecting wrong parent chain (must contain double bond), wrong numbering direction (double bond priority), missing position numbers, and incorrect suffixes for multiple double bonds.
